tweets.text:
After regional Fed presidents Kashkari and Hammack, two of the three dissenters from the April #FOMC decision who wanted to remove the "easing bias" from the Fed's statement, came out with hawkish speeches last week, the third of that group, Dallas Fed President Laurie Logan, in a speech in Texas Wednesday was the first to put a more specific timeline on when she needs to see inflation improving materially towards the 2% target saying "higher interest rates could be necessary later this year to fully restore price stability and appropriately balance both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate."

While a 2026 hike might be inferred from the wording used by others, such as  "in the near future," it is increasingly looking like we might be getting votes for hikes in the second half.

After noting that "economic activity remains strong," "consumer spending... robust," "corporate earnings [in aggregate] are going gangbusters," "financial conditions are accommodative... AI investment continues to boom," she says "conditions indicate that monetary policy is not restraining the economy."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Which sets up my post on Cleveland Fed President Hammack (an #FOMC voter in 2026 (and every two years unlike many Fed presidents) who also believes that rates are not restrictive) who in a speech in Cleveland (link below) says while for now "it’s reasonable to keep rates steady https://t.co/ZYRufiJryG
tweets.full_text:
After regional Fed presidents Kashkari and Hammack, two of the three dissenters from the April #FOMC decision who wanted to remove the "easing bias" from the Fed's statement, came out with hawkish speeches last week, the third of that group, Dallas Fed President Laurie Logan, in a speech in Texas Wednesday was the first to put a more specific timeline on when she needs to see inflation improving materially towards the 2% target saying "higher interest rates could be necessary later this year to fully restore price stability and appropriately balance both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate."

While a 2026 hike might be inferred from the wording used by others, such as  "in the near future," it is increasingly looking like we might be getting votes for hikes in the second half.

After noting that "economic activity remains strong," "consumer spending... robust," "corporate earnings [in aggregate] are going gangbusters," "financial conditions are accommodative... AI investment continues to boom," she says "conditions indicate that monetary policy is not restraining the economy."

tweets.text:
BoA on NFP: After job growth surged in Mar and Apr, we look for May NFP to print at a solid, above consensus 95k (private: 100k), which would be comfortably above breakeven. 

Education & health should continue to lead, followed by trade & transport and leisure & hospitality, but it is likely we get some job growth broadening. 

Risks are tilted to the upside, with claims still benign, strong weekly ADP data and early World Cup hiring.
tweets.full_text:
BoA on NFP: After job growth surged in Mar and Apr, we look for May NFP to print at a solid, above consensus 95k (private: 100k), which would be comfortably above breakeven. 

Education & health should continue to lead, followed by trade & transport and leisure & hospitality, but it is likely we get some job growth broadening. 

Risks are tilted to the upside, with claims still benign, strong weekly ADP data and early World Cup hiring.

tweets.text:
Goldman: Despite the sharp rise in oil prices, company commentary and results suggest that the consumer remained healthy in the first quarter. 

Sales growth among consumer-facing companies improved—sales increased 9% year-over-year among the median S&P 500 consumer discretionary company and 5% for the median consumer staples company. 

That message from company results is corroborated by the official statistics and alternative data: Exhibit 2 shows that monthly measures of nominal consumer spending—including those that exclude gasoline purchases—have continued to grow at roughly the same rate for the last few months.
tweets.full_text:
Goldman: Despite the sharp rise in oil prices, company commentary and results suggest that the consumer remained healthy in the first quarter. 

Sales growth among consumer-facing companies improved—sales increased 9% year-over-year among the median S&P 500 consumer discretionary company and 5% for the median consumer staples company. 

That message from company results is corroborated by the official statistics and alternative data: Exhibit 2 shows that monthly measures of nominal consumer spending—including those that exclude gasoline purchases—have continued to grow at roughly the same rate for the last few months.

tweets.text:
BoA: Spending on BoA cards (credit+debit) up +5.2% y/y in the week through May 30th, decelerating slightly from +5.7% the prior week with ex-gasoline spending (gasoline was +25.2% y/y) remaining very healthy at +4.1% (+4.5% the prior week and +3.5% the week before that). 

Gains continued to be led by electronics, up +19.1% (after >+20% the prior two weeks). Airlines and general merchandise both up over 6% while clothing and dining out were up around 3.5%. 

More categories down y/y though after only two (department stores and entertainment) the prior week. Spending continues to be led by higher income households.

tweets.text:
BoA continues: So, why [if the Fed's models say the Fed should be hiking] isn’t the Fed raising rates? 

Former Fed chair Powell has been explicit that the  Fed would be hiking if it weren’t looking through tariff-driven inflation as a one-off increase in price levels that should roll off the y/y rate over the next few quarters. 

We estimate that tariffs have added nearly 70bp to y/y core PCE inflation (Exhibit 2). Therefore, underlying inflation is still above target, but not egregiously so. If we plug our ex-tariff inflation estimates into the above Taylor Rules, we find that policy was loose last year – justifying the rate cuts in 2H 2025 – and is now right where it “should” be (Exhibit 3). 

To be clear, the Fed does not strictly follow the Taylor Rule. But our analysis shows how  Chair Warsh can still make a strong case for staying on hold in the near term, as we expect him to do at the June press conference.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
BoA: With the Fed conversation moving from cuts to hikes, there has been renewed interest  in the Taylor Rule. 

The Fed looks at a range of Taylor Rule models that assess how policy should be set in response to inflation and activity data. The two most important models are: i) https://t.co/CGnkbQk5ju

We estimate that tariffs have added nearly 70bp to y/y core PCE inflation (Exhibit 2). Therefore, underlying inflation is still above target, but not egregiously so. If we plug our ex-tariff inflation estimates into the above Taylor Rules, we find that policy was loose last year – justifying the rate cuts in 2H 2025 – and is now right where it “should” be (Exhibit 3). 

To be clear, the Fed does not strictly follow the Taylor Rule. But our analysis shows how  Chair Warsh can still make a strong case for staying on hold in the near term, as we expect him to do at the June press conference.

tweets.text:
Goldman: The market has already priced significant relief since the Iran ceasefire. Our macro tools that decompose the joint movement of equities and bond yields into estimated “growth” and “policy” shocks that are driving the market (Exhibit 1) suggest that the market has more than fully unwound the negative growth downgrade that the market began pricing in February, but that the market is still pricing a more hawkish view of policy/inflation risks than before the start of the war.

Our baseline forecasts assume a reopening of the Strait and normalization of oil flows by the end of June, though no further sustained downside in oil prices.... Our growth pricing estimates suggest that the market is pricing US growth at 2.6%, which is above even our economists better growth outlook for 2027, so—taking these literally—our tools would suggest that the market is already pricing a more optimistic growth outlook than our baseline forecasts. 

Our interest rate forecasts [though] are dovish to market pricing (which is already priced for hikes), as are our inflation forecasts, so our baseline forecasts suggest some room for policy relief from here.
tweets.full_text:

Our baseline forecasts assume a reopening of the Strait and normalization of oil flows by the end of June, though no further sustained downside in oil prices.... Our growth pricing estimates suggest that the market is pricing US growth at 2.6%, which is above even our economists better growth outlook for 2027, so—taking these literally—our tools would suggest that the market is already pricing a more optimistic growth outlook than our baseline forecasts. 

Our interest rate forecasts [though] are dovish to market pricing (which is already priced for hikes), as are our inflation forecasts, so our baseline forecasts suggest some room for policy relief from here.

tweets.text:
Yardeni: Yesterday, we raised the caution flag, suggesting a possible pullback over the next few weeks... we view any pullback as a buying opportunity and maintain our 8250 target for the S&P 500 by year-end.
...
Might there be more FEMO ahead? Yes, but it might already be discounted by the market, making it vulnerable to even slight disappointments. A case in point is Broadcom's ... after-hours sell-off... when the company's solid earnings report was somewhat weaker than expected.

S&P 500 forward earnings is up 26.6% y/y through May. That's as good as FEMO gets historically, with the exception of post-recession recoveries (chart).

tweets.text:
$AVGO Semiconductor seller Broadcom, the 6th largest component of the S&P 500 with an over $2 trillion market cap is shedding -14.5% of that this morning, the biggest drop in 16 months, after a disappointing forecast for AI revenue seeing $16 billion for the current quarter vs expectations for $17.2 billion. 

Chief Executive Officer Hock Tan said Broadcom will sell $56 billion in AI chips in the fiscal year that ends in October. That forecast fell short of the average estimate of $57.6 billion. 

Total revenue in the period ending in July will be about $29.4 billion, Broadcom said. While analysts estimated $28.6 billion on average, whisper numbers were higher.

Still with a recent run-up the decline just takes shares back to where they were two weeks ago. 

In the fiscal second quarter, which ended May 3, sales rose 48% to $22.2 billion. Analysts had projected $22.1 billion on average. Earnings climbed to $2.44 a share, excluding some items. That compared with an estimate of $2.39.

AI semiconductor revenue was $10.8 billion, compared with an average estimate of $10.7 billion.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
🇺🇸 #Broadcom Slides After AI #Chip Outlook Disappoints Investors - Bloomberg
https://t.co/cL1zWCtXY0
tweets.full_text:
$AVGO Semiconductor seller Broadcom, the 6th largest component of the S&P 500 with an over $2 trillion market cap is shedding -14.5% of that this morning, the biggest drop in 16 months, after a disappointing forecast for AI revenue seeing $16 billion for the current quarter vs expectations for $17.2 billion. 

Chief Executive Officer Hock Tan said Broadcom will sell $56 billion in AI chips in the fiscal year that ends in October. That forecast fell short of the average estimate of $57.6 billion. 

Total revenue in the period ending in July will be about $29.4 billion, Broadcom said. While analysts estimated $28.6 billion on average, whisper numbers were higher.

Still with a recent run-up the decline just takes shares back to where they were two weeks ago. 

In the fiscal second quarter, which ended May 3, sales rose 48% to $22.2 billion. Analysts had projected $22.1 billion on average. Earnings climbed to $2.44 a share, excluding some items. That compared with an estimate of $2.39.

AI semiconductor revenue was $10.8 billion, compared with an average estimate of $10.7 billion.

In May, AI led all reasons for job cuts for the third month, with 38,579 announced cuts.

Through May 2026, U.S. employers have announced 80,472 planned hires, narrowly topping the 79,741 announced at this point in 2025. https://t.co/nsVck3Tzjp

tweets.text:
U.S. employers announce 97,006 job cuts in May, rising 16% M/M

AI drives the most layoff announcements since January

So far this year, employers have announced 397,755 cuts, down 43% from the 696,309 announced through the first five months of 2025 https://t.co/Eo98QRmcoh

tweets.text:
Elon Musk is set to become the world's first Trillionaire.
Crazy stats in today's @WSJ article:
https://t.co/1ItqzK2VNb
Musk’s wealth includes 
-$538 billion for his pre-IPO stake in SpaceX, 
-$167 billion for his stake in Tesla, 
-$150 billion or so for stock options in those companies he could exercise just about any time.
-$5 billion apiece for The Boring Company, which drills tunnels, and Neuralink, the brain-implant firm he founded, and 
-$104 billion in property, aircraft and other investments and assets.
-He has made $3.6 million/hour over 31 years.

tweets.text:
Google ($GOOG) upsized its first equity offering in more than two decades to $85 billion amid strong investor demand, ending a decade of buybacks.
This adds to the equity issuance already slated by the coming mega-IPOs. https://t.co/WvvjuyIDC7

tweets.text: Goldman estimates that the boost from commodity price increases to year-over-year core PCE inflation will peak around 0.35 percentage points in Q4 2026 in the baseline scenario for oil prices. Inflation will then start to revert back towards its normalized growth trend of 2%ish, matching the economic growth rate.
tweets.full_text: Goldman estimates that the boost from commodity price increases to year-over-year core PCE inflation will peak around 0.35 percentage points in Q4 2026 in the baseline scenario for oil prices. Inflation will then start to revert back towards its normalized growth trend of 2%ish, matching the economic growth rate.

tweets.text:
Quarterly earnings growth, both in the US and globally, continues to accelerate.  You don't normally see this level of EPS increase, historically speaking,  unless you are coming out of a recession.  
h/t @ISABELNET_SA https://t.co/aVlHhskfzk
